subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Every human being who has ever lived - your parents, your grandparents, the scholars and travellers of long ago, and you yourself - began life in the same remarkable way: as a single, tiny cell smaller than a grain of sand on the beach at Berbera. From that one cell grew a whole person, with a beating heart, a thinking brain, and hands able to write and to give. In this unit you will study, with care and respect, how new human life begins and grows. This is one of the great wonders of creation, and studying it is a way of reflecting on how carefully we have been made.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 8 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you should be able to do each of the following. Keep this list close, and tick each item off when you are confident.
Name and state the function of the main parts of the male and female reproductive systems.
Describe the physical and emotional changes that happen to boys and girls during puberty and adolescence.
Explain why good hygiene, cleanliness and self-care become especially important during puberty.
Describe the menstrual cycle, including menstruation, the release of an egg cell, and the building and breaking down of the uterus lining.
Explain fertilisation as the joining (fusion) of the nucleus of a sperm cell with the nucleus of an egg cell.
Describe how the fertilised egg develops into an embryo and then a fetus, and the role of the placenta, umbilical cord and amniotic fluid.
Outline the main stages of pregnancy (gestation) and of birth.
Describe how a young person can stay healthy during adolescence through hygiene, balanced nutrition, rest and self-respect.

Before we begin, study this table of the most important words in the unit. These are the correct scientific and medical terms, used by doctors everywhere. Learning them helps you speak about the body clearly and respectfully. You will meet each one again in the lesson.

concepts.title: You are also at an age when your own body is changing.…
concepts.explanation: You are also at an age when your own body is changing. Between about eleven and sixteen years, every young person passes through a stage called puberty, when the body matures from a child's into an adult's. These changes are completely normal, healthy and part of growing up - yet many young people feel confused or worried about them simply because no one explained clearly what is happening and why. This unit will explain it clearly, calmly and respectfully, so that you understand your own body and can look after…
concepts.example: We will study this topic the way a doctor or a scientist does: carefully, factually and with dignity. Our bodies are an amanah - a trust given to us to look after well - and part of caring for that trust is…

concepts.title: We will study this topic the way a doctor or a scientist…
concepts.explanation: We will study this topic the way a doctor or a scientist does: carefully, factually and with dignity. Our bodies are an amanah - a trust given to us to look after well - and part of caring for that trust is understanding how it works and keeping it clean and healthy. You will learn the names and jobs of the reproductive organs, the changes of puberty, the monthly cycle in the female body, how a new life begins, how a baby develops and is born, and how to stay clean, strong and healthy through these years.…
concepts.example: Did You Know? A newborn baby begins life as a single cell, yet by the time it is born it is made of around two trillion cells - that is 2,000,000,000,000 cells - all working together, all grown from that one starting…

concepts.title: Did You Know? A newborn baby begins life as a single cell,…
concepts.explanation: Did You Know? A newborn baby begins life as a single cell, yet by the time it is born it is made of around two trillion cells - that is 2,000,000,000,000 cells - all working together, all grown from that one starting cell in about nine months. Nothing that human beings build - not the tallest tower nor the finest machine - grows itself as a living baby does.

explorations.prompt: The male sex cell is called the: (a) egg cell (b) sperm cell (c) zygote (d) embryo
explorations.answer: (b) sperm cell. The sperm cell is the male sex cell, made in the testes; the egg cell is the female sex cell.

workedExamples.title: Working With the Menstrual Cycle
workedExamples.prompt: Some questions in this unit ask you to work with the timing of the menstrual cycle. Let us work through an example carefully, step by step, the way you should set out your own answers.
workedExamples.solution: Problem: Maryam's menstrual cycle is a regular 28 days. Her most recent period began on the 3rd day of the month. (a) On about which day of the month is she likely to release an egg cell (ovulate)? (b) On about which day of the month would her next period be expected to begin? Step 1 - Recall the pattern of the cycle. Day 1 of the cycle is the first day of the period. In a 28-day cycle, ovulation happens at roughly the middle - about Day 14. The next period begins when the whole cycle of 28 days is complete, that…

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: Egg cells are made and released by the: (a) uterus (b) oviducts (c) ovaries (d) cervix
workedExamples.solution: (c) ovaries. The ovaries store and release egg cells and make the hormone oestrogen.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: The organ in which a baby develops during pregnancy is the: (a) ovary (b) uterus (c) oviduct (d) placenta
workedExamples.solution: (b) uterus. The muscular uterus (womb) is where a baby develops during pregnancy.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: The stage when a child's body matures into an adult body is called: (a) gestation (b) fertilisation (c) puberty (d) ovulation
workedExamples.solution: (c) puberty. Puberty is the stage when the body matures into an adult body able to reproduce.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: The main male hormone is: (a) oestrogen (b) testosterone (c) insulin (d) adrenaline
workedExamples.solution: (b) testosterone. Testosterone is the main male hormone, made in the testes; oestrogen is the main female hormone.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Which change happens to BOTH boys and girls during puberty? (a) the voice deepens (b) breasts develop (c) a growth spurt (d) facial hair grows
workedExamples.solution: (c) a growth spurt. A growth spurt happens to both; deepening voice and facial hair are boys' changes and breast development is a girls' change.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: Fertilisation is the joining of the nucleus of a sperm cell with the nucleus of: (a) an egg cell (b) the uterus lining (c) the placenta (d) a red blood cell
workedExamples.solution: (a) an egg cell. Fertilisation is the joining of the nucleus of a sperm cell with the nucleus of an egg cell.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: In a typical 28-day menstrual cycle, an egg cell is usually released about: (a) Day 1 (b) Day 7 (c) Day 14 (d) Day 28
workedExamples.solution: (c) Day 14. In a 28-day cycle, ovulation happens at roughly the middle, about Day 14.

practice.prompt: The male sex cell is called the: (a) egg cell (b) sperm cell (c) zygote (d) embryo
practice.answer: (b) sperm cell. The sperm cell is the male sex cell, made in the testes; the egg cell is the female sex cell.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Egg cells are made and released by the: (a) uterus (b) oviducts (c) ovaries (d) cervix
practice.answer: (c) ovaries. The ovaries store and release egg cells and make the hormone oestrogen.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The organ in which a baby develops during pregnancy is the: (a) ovary (b) uterus (c) oviduct (d) placenta
practice.answer: (b) uterus. The muscular uterus (womb) is where a baby develops during pregnancy.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The stage when a child's body matures into an adult body is called: (a) gestation (b) fertilisation (c) puberty (d) ovulation
practice.answer: (c) puberty. Puberty is the stage when the body matures into an adult body able to reproduce.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The main male hormone is: (a) oestrogen (b) testosterone (c) insulin (d) adrenaline
practice.answer: (b) testosterone. Testosterone is the main male hormone, made in the testes; oestrogen is the main female hormone.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which change happens to BOTH boys and girls during puberty? (a) the voice deepens (b) breasts develop (c) a growth spurt (d) facial hair grows
practice.answer: (c) a growth spurt. A growth spurt happens to both; deepening voice and facial hair are boys' changes and breast development is a girls' change.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Fertilisation is the joining of the nucleus of a sperm cell with the nucleus of: (a) an egg cell (b) the uterus lining (c) the placenta (d) a red blood cell
practice.answer: (a) an egg cell. Fertilisation is the joining of the nucleus of a sperm cell with the nucleus of an egg cell.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: In a typical 28-day menstrual cycle, an egg cell is usually released about: (a) Day 1 (b) Day 7 (c) Day 14 (d) Day 28
practice.answer: (c) Day 14. In a 28-day cycle, ovulation happens at roughly the middle, about Day 14.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Fertilisation normally takes place in the: (a) ovary (b) oviduct (c) vagina (d) cervix
practice.answer: (b) oviduct. Fertilisation normally takes place in the oviduct (egg tube), not the uterus.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The single cell formed at fertilisation is called a: (a) fetus (b) zygote (c) placenta (d) gamete
practice.answer: (b) zygote. The single new cell formed at fertilisation is called a zygote.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The developing baby is called a fetus from about: (a) week 1 (b) week 8 until birth (c) only at birth (d) week 40 only
practice.answer: (b) week 8 until birth. The developing baby is an embryo for about the first eight weeks, then a fetus until birth.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The organ that passes oxygen and food from the mother to the baby is the: (a) uterus (b) cervix (c) placenta (d) ovary
practice.answer: (c) placenta. The placenta passes oxygen and food from mother to baby and removes waste.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Observe and record
steps: Choose one idea from this unit.
Observe an example of it at home.
Record what you see with a drawing and labels.
Share your recording with your family.

terms: The egg tube carrying an egg towards the uterus; where fertilisation happens.
The muscular organ where a baby develops during pregnancy.
The ring of muscle at the base of the uterus; opens during birth.
The passage from the uterus to the outside; the birth passage.
The stage when a child's body matures into an adult body (about 11-16 years).
commonMistakes: Thinking puberty happens at the same age for all
Believing menstruation is an illness or 'unclean'
It is a healthy, normal monthly process
Saying the mother's and baby's blood mix
They flow close but stay separate
Thinking the baby eats and breathes for itself
Its lungs and gut are not used yet
Saying fertilisation happens in the uterus
It happens earlier, in the egg tube

realProblems.prompt: Apply what you have learned to real situations. Answer with care and in full sentences.
realProblems.answer: During puberty the sweat glands become more active because of the hormones controlling puberty, so Amina sweats more. This is completely normal. The healthy habit she should build is washing her body daily with clean water and soap and wearing clean clothes, to stay fresh and prevent body odour.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: During puberty the sweat glands become more active because of the hormones controlling puberty, so Amina sweats more. This is completely normal. The healthy habit she should build is washing her body daily with clean water and soap and wearing clean clothes, to stay fresh and prevent body odour.

realProblems.prompt: Amina is twelve and has noticed that she sweats more than she used to and needs to wash more often. Explain, using the science of puberty, why this is happening and what healthy habit she should build.
realProblems.answer: Day 1 of the cycle is the first day of the period, so the 5th is Day 1. In a 28-day cycle, ovulation is about Day 14. Counting from the 5th: Day 14 falls on the 18th of the month (5 + 13 = 18). So Khadija is likely to release an egg cell around the 18th.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Day 1 of the cycle is the first day of the period, so the 5th is Day 1. In a 28-day cycle, ovulation is about Day 14. Counting from the 5th: Day 14 falls on the 18th of the month (5 + 13 = 18). So Khadija is likely to release an egg cell around the 18th.

realProblems.prompt: Khadija's regular menstrual cycle is 28 days, and her last period began on the 5th of the month. On about which day of the month is she likely to release an egg cell? Show your reasoning.
realProblems.answer: The developing baby receives oxygen and food from the mother's blood through the placenta and umbilical cord, and its waste is carried away the same way. So whatever the mother eats and drinks affects what reaches her baby: good, balanced food and clean, boiled water help the baby grow healthily,…
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The developing baby receives oxygen and food from the mother's blood through the placenta and umbilical cord, and its waste is carried away the same way. So whatever the mother eats and drinks affects what reaches her baby: good, balanced food and clean, boiled water help the baby grow healthily,…

realProblems.prompt: A pregnant mother in Hargeisa is advised by her grandmother to eat well, rest, and drink only clean, boiled water. Using what you know about the placenta, explain why the mother's health and diet affect her developing baby.
realProblems.answer: The baby cannot breathe or eat for itself inside the mother. Instead, it receives oxygen and dissolved food from the mother's blood through the placenta and the umbilical cord, and its waste is carried back the same way. Its lungs and digestive system do not do these jobs until after birth.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.

Its lungs and digestive system do not do these jobs until after birth.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the male and female sex cells, and state where each one is made.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The male sex cell is the sperm cell, made in the testes. The female sex cell is the egg cell (ovum), made in (and released from) the ovaries.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: State two jobs of the ovaries.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The ovaries (1) store and release egg cells, and (2) make the female hormone oestrogen. (Either two of these.)

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Give two changes of puberty that happen to both boys and girls.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Accept any two shared changes, such as: a growth spurt, underarm hair, more sweat and body odour, oilier skin, or emotional changes.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain what happens during menstruation and why it is a normal, healthy process.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: During menstruation the thickened lining of the uterus, no longer needed because no baby is developing, breaks down and leaves the body through the vagina. It is a normal, healthy monthly sign that the female body is working well - not an illness.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Define fertilisation precisely, using the word 'nucleus'.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Fertilisation is the joining (fusion) of the nucleus of a sperm cell with the nucleus of an egg cell, forming a single new cell.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Put these stages in the correct order and name what changes at each: fetus, zygote, embryo.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Correct order: zygote (the single cell at fertilisation), then embryo (first eight weeks, main parts forming), then fetus (from eight weeks until birth, clearly human and growing).

questions.question: The male sex cell is called the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: sperm cell. The sperm cell is the male sex cell, made in the testes; the egg cell is the female sex cell.

questions.question: Egg cells are made and released by the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: ovaries. The ovaries store and release egg cells and make the hormone oestrogen.

questions.question: The organ in which a baby develops during pregnancy is the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: uterus. The muscular uterus (womb) is where a baby develops during pregnancy.

questions.question: The stage when a child's body matures into an adult body is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: puberty. Puberty is the stage when the body matures into an adult body able to reproduce.

questions.question: The main male hormone is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: testosterone. Testosterone is the main male hormone, made in the testes; oestrogen is the main female hormone.

questions.question: Which change happens to BOTH boys and girls during puberty?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a growth spurt. A growth spurt happens to both; deepening voice and facial hair are boys' changes and breast development is a girls' change.

questions.question: Fertilisation is the joining of the nucleus of a sperm cell with the nucleus of:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: an egg cell. Fertilisation is the joining of the nucleus of a sperm cell with the nucleus of an egg cell.

questions.question: In a typical 28-day menstrual cycle, an egg cell is usually released about:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Day 14. In a 28-day cycle, ovulation happens at roughly the middle, about Day 14.

questions.question: Fertilisation normally takes place in the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: oviduct. Fertilisation normally takes place in the oviduct (egg tube), not the uterus.

questions.question: The single cell formed at fertilisation is called a:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: zygote. The single new cell formed at fertilisation is called a zygote.

questions.question: The developing baby is called a fetus from about:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: week 8 until birth. The developing baby is an embryo for about the first eight weeks, then a fetus until birth.

questions.question: The organ that passes oxygen and food from the mother to the baby is the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: placenta. The placenta passes oxygen and food from mother to baby and removes waste.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you should be able to do each of the following. Keep this list close, and tick each item off when you are confident.
I can name and state the function of the main parts of the male and female reproductive systems.
I can describe the physical and emotional changes that happen to boys and girls during puberty and adolescence.
I can explain why good hygiene, cleanliness and self-care become especially important during puberty.
I can describe the menstrual cycle, including menstruation, the release of an egg cell, and the building and breaking down of the uterus lining.
I can explain fertilisation as the joining (fusion) of the nucleus of a sperm cell with the nucleus of an egg cell.
I can describe how the fertilised egg develops into an embryo and then a fetus, and the role of the placenta, umbilical cord and amniotic fluid.
I can outline the main stages of pregnancy (gestation) and of birth.
